coredata.dc:title: Full spectrum driven SCR removal of NO over hierarchical CeVO4/attapulgite nanocomposite with high resistance to SO2 and H2O
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiazhang
Wang, Zhendong
Shi, Haiyang
Dai, Da
Zuo, Shixiang
Yao, Chao
Ni, Chaoying
coredata.dc:description: Removal of hazardous NO at low temperature via photo-assisted selective catalytic reduction (photo-SCR) strategy is promising, however fully harvesting of solar energy and achieving high SO2/H2O tolerance still remain a challenge. Herein, the phosphoric acid modified natural attapulgite(P-ATP) was employed as a matrix to immobilize CeVO4 by microwave hydrothermal method. Results show that P-ATP provides abundant active sites facilitating the in situ grow of CeVO4 nanorods on its surface which hierarchically construct a dendritic-like photocatalyst. The near-infrared (NIR) light is upconverted to visible and UV light through CeVO4 which not only broaden the absorption range of solar light, but also build Z-scheme heterostructure with P-ATP enhancing the redox potential of charge carriers. The CeVO4/P-ATP nanocomposite can reach as high as 92 % for NO conversion under full-spectrum solar irradiation, while retaining nearly 60 % conversion under NIR light. Moreover, the catalyst exhibits outstanding tolerance with SO2 and H2O due to the presence of Ce species which can prevent NH3 from being sulfated, while ATP prevent catalyst from being corroded by H2O. This work may open up a new window for full-spectrum driven SCR of NO based on cost-effective mineral catalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NO removal
Photo-SCR
Attapulgite
CeVO4
Upconversion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP.
TEM of ATP (a), P-ATP (b), CeVO4/P-ATP (c), HRTEM (d), SAED (e) and EDS (f) of CeVO4/P-ATP.
FT-IR spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP.
XPS spectra of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP survey spectrum(a), Ce 3d(b), V 2p(c) and O 1s(d).
NH3-TPD profiles of ATP, P-ATP and CeVO4/P-ATP.
H2-TPR profiles of ATP, P-ATP and CeVO4/P-ATP.
UV–vis spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP (a), corresponding Tauc plots (b), M-S plots (c) and VB-XPS spectra (d) of P-ATP and CeVO4.

Upconversion PL spectra (excitation at 780 nm) of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP (a), PL spectra (excitation at 300 nm) (b), photocurrent transient response(c) and electrochemical impedance spectra (d) of samples ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP.

Photo-SCR activity of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP under simulated solar light illumination (a), photo-SCR activity of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP under NIR light illumination (b), effect of SO2 and H2O on the catalytic activities of CeVO4/P-ATP at 150 °C (c) and stability test of the CeVO4/P-ATP at 150 °C for 30 h (d), photo-SCR with and without solar light irradiation over CeVO4/P-ATP catalyst (e), N2 selectivity of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP (f).
Band configuration and transfer pathways of photogenerated carriers before (a) and after (b) contact.
Photo-SCR mechanism under simulated solar light illumination (a), under NIR illumination (b).

head.ce:title: Full spectrum driven SCR removal of NO over hierarchical CeVO4/attapulgite nanocomposite with high resistance to SO2 and H2O

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Removal of hazardous NO at low temperature via photo-assisted selective catalytic reduction (photo-SCR) strategy is promising, however fully harvesting of solar energy and achieving high SO2/H2O tolerance still remain a challenge. Herein, the phosphoric acid modified natural attapulgite(P-ATP) was employed as a matrix to immobilize CeVO4 by microwave hydrothermal method. Results show that P-ATP provides abundant active sites facilitating the in situ grow of CeVO4 nanorods on its surface which hierarchically construct a dendritic-like photocatalyst. The near-infrared (NIR) light is upconverted to visible and UV light through CeVO4 which not only broaden the absorption range of solar light, but also build Z-scheme heterostructure with P-ATP enhancing the redox potential of charge carriers. The CeVO4/P-ATP nanocomposite can reach as high as 92 % for NO conversion under full-spectrum solar irradiation, while retaining nearly 60 % conversion under NIR light. Moreover, the catalyst exhibits outstanding tolerance with SO2 and H2O due to the presence of Ce species which can prevent NH3 from being sulfated, while ATP prevent catalyst from being corroded by H2O. This work may open up a new window for full-spectrum driven SCR of NO based on cost-effective mineral catalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nitrogen oxides (NO) bring a series of hazardous effects on ecosystem and human health, such as acid rain, photochemical smog and respiratory diseases (Shi et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018a). Therefore, the treatment of NO has become particularly urgent. At present, V-W-Ti catalyst is widely used in industry to remove NO by selective catalyst reduction (SCR) method. However, this technique has drawbacks including relatively high (300–400 °C) reaction temperature, environmental toxicity, and poor resistance to SO2 and H2O (Arfaoui et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019). Therefore, to meet the strict requirements, it is inevitable to develop new method for efficient removal of NO.

Photocatalytic conversion is a promising denitrification technology with low energy consumption, mild reaction conditions and less secondary pollution (Huang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016a). In particular, the photo assisted or driven SCR (photo-SCR) has an unparalleled advantage in terms of N2 selectivity and NO conversion rate in the low temperature range. Tanaka group has developed photo-SCR for NO removal by taking advantage of TiO2 or doped TiO2 as catalyst, but only 4 % ultraviolet light of solar light can be absorbed under room temperature operation (Yamamoto et al., 2015a,b), since the bandgap of TiO2 is very wide (3.2 eV), which is almost non-responsive for the visible light or NIR light in solar spectrum. Previously, various metal-doped rare earth perovskite nanocomposites loaded on ATP have been fabricated for photo-SCR of NO in our group (Li et al., 2017, 2018a). However, the composite can only harvest visible light, while the NIR occupying nearly half of the solar region is still not accessible, thereby leading to the inefficient use of solar energy. Therefore, in order to improve the utilization of solar light, it is essential to develop new photocatalyst which can harvest full spectrum.

Up to date, some narrow bandgap materials, such as black phosphorus, carbon quantum dots and perovskite quantum dots, have the characteristics of wide-edge spectrum absorption for sunlight (Wang et al., 2016b; Wang et al., 2019a; Yu et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the synthesis conditions of such semiconductors are extremely harsh and they are always unstable under catalytic reactions. Alternatively, some upconversion luminescence materials, such as fluorides, sulfur compounds and halides, can absorb NIR photons and emit UV or visible light by doping rare earth ions, which may achieve full spectrum utilization of solar light (Chen et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2018; Reddy et al., 2019). In particular, CeVO4 has demonstrated remarkable upconversion of NIR photons due to its abundant energy levels (Reddy et al., 2019). It is essential to build heterojunction with appropriate support to prevent the accumulation of CeVO4 particles and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, which may directly affects the catalytic performance (Zhao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018b).

As a magnesium-rich silicate clay mineral, ATP owns 1-D fibrous structure with large specific surface area and adequate active sites (Li et al., 2018b). The ATP contains iron oxide endowing it good response in the ultraviolet region. Liu et al. achieves visible light response absorption by dye-sensitized ATP for hydrogen production under visible light irradiation (Zhang and Liu, 2014). In our previous works, we successfully synthesized Tm3+:CeO2/carbon quantum dots/ATP upconverting luminescent material with acid modified ATP as carrier (Li et al., 2019a). The existence of abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface render it excellent property for in situ growth or assembly of active species.

In this work, the natural clay mineral ATP was initially modified by phosphoric acid, and the CeVO4 nanorods subsequently grew on the surface of P-ATP, resembling a dendritic morphology via microwave hydrothermal method. The NO removal performance was investigated by rational design of Z-scheme CeVO4/P-ATP heterostructure which not only effectively separated the photogenerated charge carriers but also enhanced the redox potential. The NIR light range was absorbed and upconverted to realize full spectrum response, which broadened the utilization range of solar light. In addition, the resistance of SO2 and H2O as well as stability under long time running was explored.
Cerium nitrate, ammonium metavanadate, phosphoric acid and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Attapulgite clay was obtained from Xuyi, China.

First, 2.0 g ATP was dissolved in 100 mL 30 % phosphoric acid solution under ultrasonic waves. Then, the mixture was placed in a water bath at 70 °C for 10 h. The solid was washed 3 times by deionized water. After drying at 80 °C for 15 h, P-ATP was obtained.

2.5 mol EDTA and 2 mol Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were completely dissolved in a 50 mL beaker under stirring. Then, 2 mol NH4VO3 was added to the above solution and 1 M aqueous ammonia solution was dropwise added into the above solution for adjusting the pH to 10. Furthermore, 1.0 g P-ATP was added to the above solution under stirring at room temperature for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 100 mL microwave hydrothermal reactor autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 1 h. After naturally cooled to ambient temperature, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water. Finally, the CeVO4/P-ATP was obtained by drying at 80 °C for 8 h. The formation process can be seen in Scheme 1 . Pure CeVO4 samples were prepared in the same way without the addition of P-ATP.

The phase composition of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (D/Max 2500 PC, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) with a Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Spectrum 100 spectrometer (PE, USA). The morphology of the samples was observed by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) with an operating voltage of 200 kV, which was equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and a GATAN832 CCD. The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of the material were measured using a scanning UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured at room temperature on a fluorophotometer (LS45) with an excitation of 360 nm. The upconverted photoluminescence spectra of the prepared samples were investigated on a FLS980 series fluorescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of the samples were tested using a Quantum 2000 scanning ESCA microprobe instrument equipped with Al Kα radiation. The redox properties were performed by H2-TPR on a Micromeritics ASAP 2920 instrument. The acid property of the samples was performed by NH3-TPD on the same instrument. The photoelectrochemical tests for the Mott-Schottky plots, photocurrent and electrochemical impendence spectroscopy (EIS) were measured on the CHI 660D (Shanghai Chenhua) electrochemical work station in a standard three-electrode system containing 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. A Pt electrode was employed as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode in saturated KCl solution was used as the reference electrode, and the sample deposited on the fluorine-doped tin oxide glasses with an area of 4 cm2 was used as the working electrode. The photocurrent response experiment was equipped with a 500 W Xe lamp as the light source. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was tested at an alternating current voltage amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz.

Equipped with NO/NO2 gas analyzer (Kahn International Co., Ltd) and N2O gas analyzer (Shenzhen Beit Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd), the PFD-5000 (Hunan Huasi Instrument Co., Ltd) photothermal fixed-bed flow reactor was employed for photo-SCR of NO. The gas lines were heated up to 190 °C with electric tracing to prevent NH3NO4 formation and water condensation. The flue gas consisted of 0.1 % NH3, 0.1 % NO, 3 % O2 and N2 as balance gas. The total gas flow rate was controlled to be 150 mL/min. The solar light was simulated by a 500 W Xenon lamp as light source and the intensity of the lamp was about 50 mw/cm2. The spectrum wavelength mainly located in the range of 290−1100 nm while two optical filters were added for NIR irradiation (λ ≥ 750 nm). Two windows are opened on both sides of the reactor for illumination, and the simulated reactor is a 18 mm × 1 mm thick quartz reaction tube with a catalyst dosage of about 3 ml. Triplicate experiments were conducted for each test of the sample with relative deviation less than 5 %. During the reaction, the concentration of gas was recorded every 20 °C while the calculation formula of NO conversion was calculated as follows:
(1)
NO Conversion =
[ NO ( inlet ) -NO ( outlet ) ]
[ NO ( inlet ) ]
× 100 %
(2)
N2 selectivity = (
[ NO ] in+ [ NH3 ] in- [ NO2 ] out-2 [ N2O ] out
[ NO ] in+ [ NH3 ] in
) × 100 %

Fig.1 shows the XRD spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP. The characteristic diffraction peaks of pure ATP appear at 2θ = 8.3°, 19.8° and 27.5°, corresponding to (110), (040), and (400) crystal planes, respectively (Mu and Wang, 2016). After the modification treatment via phosphoric acid, the characteristic diffraction peaks of ATP exists, indicating that the lattice structure is maintained (Yun et al., 2007). The intensity of peak at 8.27° of P-ATP is significantly weakened, due to the partial replacement of magnesium and aluminum ions by hydrogen ions in the octahedral lattice. The typical diffraction peaks in the sample of CeVO4 appear at 18.0°, 24.0° and 32.4° corresponding to (101), (200) and (112), crystal face of CeVO4 (JCPDS 12-0757) (Zhao et al., 2016). It is observed that the characteristic diffraction peaks of P-ATP and CeVO4 both appear in the CeVO4/P-ATP nanocomposite. Compared with pure CeVO4, the characteristic peaks of the CeVO4/P-ATP nanocomposite at 24.0° and 34.3° obviously shift to larger Bragg angle. It can be rationalized that the radius of Mg2+ (0.072 nm) and Al3+ (0.053 nm) cations in P-ATP are smaller than that of Ce3+ (0.103 nm), and the cations diffusing into the CeVO4 crystal leads to lattice distortion and shrinkage, indicating that CeVO4 is successfully immobilized on the surface of P-ATP to form an intimate heterojunction (Li et al., 2018b,c), which can be further approved by the Raman analysis in Fig. S1.

The morphology of ATP, P-ATP and CeVO4/P-ATP are investigated by TEM as shown in Fig. 2 . The pure ATP presents nano fibber structure with a diameter of about 30−40 nm in Fig. 2(a). After treatment with phosphoric acid, the fibber structure is maintained, however, the surface of P-ATP becomes rough, suggesting the presence of abundant active sites as shown in Fig. 2(b) (Zhang et al., 2009). Fig. 2(c) shows that nanorod-like CeVO4 with average diameter of about 3−5 nm and length of about 15 nm grow on the P-ATP surface. Notably, a multi-level CeVO4 hierarchical structure with P-ATP may effectively enhance the absorption of light and expose more surface active sites (Li et al., 2016a). HRTEM in Fig. 2(d) shows that the lattice fringe distance d = 0.276 nm is consistent with the crystallographic plane (112) of CeVO4, which is tightly combined with the P-ATP surface to form a distinct heterojunction. Fig. 2(e) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CeVO4/P-ATP, where the diffraction rings from inside to outside correspond to (101), (200), (112) and (220) faces of CeVO4, consistent with the result of XRD. Fig. 2(f) shows the EDS spectrum of CeVO4/P-ATP, where Cu and C are mainly from the copper grid and Si, Mg, Al and Fe are from ATP.

The FT-IR spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP are shown in Fig. 3 .The characteristic peaks of ATP at 3561 cm−1, 1435 cm−1 and 1645 cm−1 are attributed to the OH stretching vibration of the adsorbed water, and the peaks at 476 cm−1, 805 cm−1 and 1002 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration of SiO, MgO and AlO groups, respectively (Zhang et al., 2016). The intensity peaks of P-ATP are clearly reduced compared with ATP, but the absorption peak at 1028 cm−1 are divided into two peaks, which may be assigned to AlOH groups, indicating that acid modification may generate more hydroxyl groups on the surface of ATP (Zhang and Liu, 2014). The peaks around 460 cm−1 and 800 cm−1 from pure CeVO4 correspond to CeO and VO structure vibrations, and the peaks around 1390 cm−1, 1645 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 are due to the OH bending vibration of adsorbed water in CeVO4. The hydroxyl groups on the surface of CeVO4/P-ATP are weaker than P-ATP at 1028 cm−1, indicating that CeVO4 is successfully supported on ATP, consistent with the XRD analysis.

Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP. Fig. 4(a) shows the survey scan of the two samples, while the Ce, V, Al, Si and O elements are all observed. Fig. 4(b) exhibits Ce 3d spectrum of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP. The binding energy locations at 882.2 and 886.0 eV are assigned to Ce 3d5/2, while those at 900.5 and 904.3 eV are assigned to Ce 3d3/2, indicating the presence of mixed valence state of Ce3+/Ce4+ (Ying et al., 2018). The Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio of CeVO4/P-ATP is 0.73 which is higher than the 0.58 of CeVO4, indicative of more defects in CeVO4/P-ATP. The peaks in the CeVO4/P-ATP shift toward higher binding energy compared with pure CeVO4, indicating that the chemical environment of Ce may be affected by the doping of Mg or Al from ATP leading to the decreased density of the electron cloud around Ce (Li et al., 2016b). Fig. 4(c) displays the V 2p spectrum of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP. The peaks near 516.8 and 524.5 eV correspond to V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2, respectively, suggesting that V species exist in +5, +4 valence in the CeVO4. The proportion of V5+ and V4+ increases from 2.85 in CeVO4 to 2.94 in CeVO4/P-ATP. In addition, the characteristic peaks of V in CeVO4/P-ATP shift to high binding energy, indicating that the doping may also affect the chemical environment of V, leading to the decreased density of electron clouds around V (Gillot et al., 2017). The reason is that the binding energy referred to the interaction between inner electrons is negatively correlated with the outer electron density due to the shielding effect. The binding energy is enhanced when the outer electron density decreases. Fig. 4(d) exhibits O 1s spectrum of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP. The peaks at 529.7 and 532.0 eV correspond to the lattice oxygen (OL) and the adsorption oxygen (OA), respectively. Obviously, the proportion of OL in pure CeVO4 is higher than OA, while the OL of CeVO4/P-ATP is lower than OA, which may be attributed to the enhanced adsorption of P-ATP. The characteristic peaks of CeVO4/P-ATP shift to higher binding energy compared with CeVO4, suggesting that the Al or Mg doping may lead to the introduction of more oxygen vacancies (Li et al., 2019b).

Fig. 5 shows the NH3-TPD curves of ATP, P-ATP and CeVO4/P-ATP samples. ATP has an obvious peak at 81 °C corresponding to the weak Brønsted acidic sites. The NH3 desorption peak of P-ATP appear near 166 °C is assigned to the weak Brønsted acidic sites while another located at 389 °C is assigned to the strong Lewis acidic sites (Li et al., 2016c). Furthermore, P-ATP shows larger NH3 desorption intensity and bigger peak area than that of ATP, which benefits the adsorption of NH3. The modification of ATP with phosphoric acid probably generates not only more abundant surface hydroxyl groups but also more acidic sites. Compared with P-ATP, the peaks in the CeVO4/P-ATP are right-shift since the growth of CeVO4 on the surface of P-ATP may cause the acidity increment (Li et al., 2018d).

The H2-TPR experiment was measured in order to investigate the thermal redox performance of the catalyst as shown in Fig. 6 . The ATP presents a reduction peak near 562 °C, which may be due to the process of Fe3+→Fe2+. P-ATP shows a reduction peak at 488 °C, attributed to the fact that acid modification changes the bonding state of Fe3+ in octahedron, leading to the decrease of reduction temperature. However, as CeVO4 is loaded on the surface, a sharper reduction peak appears near the high temperature region about 686 °C, which may be a process corresponding to V5+→V3+, indicating that CeVO4 also has certain reducibility beneficial for the synergistic catalytic ability (Ying et al., 2018).

Fig. 7 (a) presents the UV–vis spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4, and CeVO4/P-ATP samples. Pure ATP can hardly be excited under visible light, but it has weak responsive ability in the ultraviolet light region. P-ATP shows a weak absorption in the visible light band, indicating that the phosphoric acid-modified ATP can increase the absorption of light and adjust its band gap. Pure CeVO4 presents an obvious response absorption in the region of visible light, and the absorption edge appears around 650 nm. After loading CeVO4 on the surface of P-ATP, the absorption edge is expanded to the near-infrared region, which increases the light utilization efficiency, which may be due to the synergistic effect of CeVO4/P-ATP heterojunction (Ren et al., 2017). The band gap energies (Eg) of samples are calculated from Tauc plots (αhν)n/2 = A(hν − Eg), where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, n = 4 or n = 1 represent indirect bandgap semiconductor and direct bandgap semiconductor respectively, and A is a constant for the material. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the calculated band gaps of P-ATP and CeVO4 are about 3.57 eV and 1.84 eV, respectively. Fig. 7(c) shows Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots of P-ATP and CeVO4 samples, consistent with the shape of a typical n-type semiconductor curve. The plots show that the flat-band potentials (Vfb) of P-ATP and CeVO4 are determined to be 0.45 eV and −0.43 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), corresponding to a Fermi level (EF) 0.65 eV and −0.23 eV (vs NHE), respectively (Wang et al., 2018). According to the VB-XPS spectrum displayed in Fig. 7(d) and Fermi level, the valence band (VB) positions for the P-ATP and CeVO4 are calculated to be 4.21 eV and 0.75 eV, respectively. According to the formula of ECB = EVB – Eg, the corresponding conduction band (CB) positions for the P-ATP and CeVO4 can be calculated as 0.64 eV and −1.09 eV.

The upconversion luminescence spectra of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP are shown in Fig. 8 (a) with 780 nm as the excitation light wavelength. The emission light near 281, 488, 535, and 638 nm appears, indicating that CeVO4 can upconvert NIR light to ultraviolet light and visible light. It is reported that the luminescence property of CeVO4 material is attributed to its absorption of wide near-infrared photons and blackbody radiation (Chen et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). The PL spectra of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP samples with 300 nm excitation light are shown in Fig. 8(b). ATP and P-ATP show high PL peak intensity, which means high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers (Zhang et al., 2017). However, the peak intensity of CeVO4/P-ATP sample is significantly reduced, indicating that the recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers is greatly suppressed after growth of CeVO4 nanorods on the surface of P-ATP. The photoelectrochemical properties of the four samples are tested as shown in Fig. 8(c) and (d). Fig. 8(c) exhibits the photocurrent transient response under visible light illumination. The CeVO4/P-ATP sample exhibits higher current density than that of other samples, which may be due to the construction of Z-Scheme between CeVO4 and P-ATP leading to the high separation of electrons and holes, indicating that photogenerated e−-h+ migration efficiency is remarkably enhanced due to their synergistic effect. Fig. 8(d) reveals that the arc radius of the EIS spectrum of a CeVO4/P-ATP sample is overwhelmingly smaller than others, reflecting a lower resistance value and a faster interfacial charge migration.

Fig. 9 (a) shows the NO conversion of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP samples under simulated solar light illumination. The pure ATP possesses only about 20 % NO conversion while the P-ATP reaches about 30 % attributed to the reason that the acid sites, light absorption, specific surface area and pore volume of P-ATP have been significantly increased compared to the pure ATP as demonstrated in NH3-TPD, UV–vis and BET analysis, respectively. The pristine CeVO4 sample has nearly 70 % NO conversion, while the conversion of CeVO4/P-ATP reaches nearly 92 % despite that the surface area is lower (Fig. S2), which may be attributed to the synergistic effect between CeVO4 and P-ATP with respect to the formation of Z-scheme heterostructure of CeVO4/P-ATP enhancing the redox potential of charge carriers. Fig. 9(b) shows the NO conversion of CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP samples in the presence of NIR filter (>750 nm). The NO conversion of the two samples is significantly reduced as compared with Fig. 9(a). The CeVO4/P-ATP still possesses almost 60 % of NO conversion under the illumination of NIR light, since CeVO4 can absorb and convert the NIR light to UV–vis light.

The poisoning effect of SO2 and H2O over P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP samples is tested at 150 °C by introducing 100 ppm SO2 and 5 % H2O into the mixture gas. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the NO conversion does not show obviously decrease with SO2 and H2O introduction, suggesting that P-ATP has certain contribution for resistance of SO2 and H2O. The NO conversion of the CeVO4 sample is significantly reduced from 71 % to 55 %. This phenomenon is due to the formation of (NH4)2SO4 and NH4HSO4 by SO2 and NH3, which easily deposits on the catalyst surface and blocked active site, thereby reducing photo-SCR activity (Cai et al., 2016). However, the poisoning effect over CeVO4/P-ATP samples is obviously inhibited under the same conditions whose photo-SCR activity is recovered to about 85 % after stopping the introduction SO2 and H2O, indicating that the prepared nanocomposite catalyst has good sulfur and water resistance. The results indicate that the presence of Ce species can prevent NH3 from being sulfated and ATP prevent catalyst from being corroded by H2O as revealed in our previous work (Li et al., 2016c). Fig. 9(d) shows the denitrification stability of CeVO4/P-ATP catalyst at 150 °C. During 30 h continuous test, only about 3.2 % of the activity decreased, suggesting excellent running stability. In order to investigate the irradiation effect on the conversion of NO, blank test was conducted without the light source using CeVO4/P-ATP as catalyst. As shown in Fig. 9(e), the NO conversion only reaches about 56 % without light irradiation much lower that of light on, manifesting that the CeVO4/P-ATP has better deNOx performance due to the enhancement of photocatalytic effect compared with traditional SCR. The N2 selectivity curves of ATP, P-ATP, CeVO4 and CeVO4/P-ATP are shown in Fig. 9(f). The N2 selectivity of ATP and P-ATP is quite close around 27 %. The CeVO4/P-ATP achieves as much higher as 99 % compared with CeVO4 which is only 56 %. It should be noted that the N2 selectivity slightly decreases when the reaction temperature is above 150 °C possibly due to the production of N2O and NO2 byproducts.

Fig. 10 (a) shows the energy band alignment of CeVO4 and P-ATP before contact. Since the CeVO4 possesses higher Fermi level than P-ATP before contact, the free electrons of CeVO4 can be transferred to P-ATP until their EF reaches equilibrium, as presented in Fig. 10(b). After contact, the energy band edge of CeVO4 with high Fermi level is bended upward toward the interface with positively charged, while the P-ATP with low Fermi level is bended downward with negatively charged at the interface, thus forming a built-in electric field with band bending (Wang et al., 2019b).

The schematic photo-SCR under simulated sunlight for CeVO4/P-ATP is proposed in Fig. 11 (a). Both photogenerated electrons and holes can be produced after excitation by simulated sunlight. The existence of internal electric field facilitates the recombination of photogenerated electrons in the CB of P-ATP with photogenerated holes in the VB of CeVO4, thereby constructing a Z-scheme charge transfer mode (Meng et al., 2019). To verify the Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism, the DMPO-ESR spin-trap test determining the active radicals (
OH) was carried out. As shown in Fig. S3, obvious signals of
OH are detected under light irradiation suggesting that the existed VB potential is larger than 1.99 eV for oxidizing H2O into
OH, which exclude the traditional heterojunction system since the VB potential of CeVO4 is only 0.75 eV. In Fig. 11(b), under NIR light illumination, CeVO4 can absorb near-infrared photons and emit UV light and visible light by up-conversion process, which can both excite two parts and generate electrons and holes consistent with Fig. 11(a). However, the production of electrons and holes involved in the photo-SCR of NO is largely reduced.

The mechanism for photo-SCR of NO is proposed based on the above analysis. Initially, NH3 molecular is adsorbed by the acidic sites of P-ATP, thereby producing NH2 − groups (Gillot et al., 2016). Next, under light irradiation, P-ATP and CeVO4 are excited to generate photogenerated electrons and holes, and the photogenerated electrons in the CB of P-ATP are quenched in the VB of CeVO4 by the built-in electric field. Secondly, the increment of adsorption oxygen (OA) is favorable for oxidizing Ce3+ into Ce4+ according to the analysis of O1s in XPS, while the Ce4+ can be reduced to Ce3+ via the photogenerated electrons remaining in CB of CeVO4. Thirdly, the holes left in the VB of P-ATP are captured by NH2 − and oxidized to produce
NH2, which is approved by the in-situ EPR measurements shown in Fig. S4. With light irradiation, new signals appear at g = 2.005 and 2.01 attributing to the generation of the
NH2 radicals (Li et al., 2018a). Finally,
NH2 reacts with NO to form the unstable intermediate NH2NO, which is further decomposed into N2 and H2O (Li et al., 2019b). The photo-SCR of NO process is as follows:
(1) NH3 + P-ATP → NH2 −
(2) CeVO4/P-ATP + hv → CeVO4 (e−) + P-ATP (h+)
(3) Ce3+ + O2 → Ce4+
(4) Ce4+ + e− → Ce3+
(5) NH2 − + h+ +
NH2
(6)
NH2 + NO → N2 + H2O

The phosphoric acid modified natural ATP supported CeVO4 nanocomposite is successfully synthesized by microwave hydrothermal method. The nanorod CeVO4 in-situ grows on the surface of P-ATP to form hierarchical dendritic-like CeVO4/P-ATP photocatalyst, up-converts NIR light to visible and UV light, and therefore facilitates the absorption and utilization of full solar spectrum. Meanwhile, the photogenerated charge carriers migrate following the Z-scheme mechanism, effectively promoting the separation of electron-holes and enhancing the redox potential. Below 100 °C, the NO conversion rate achieves the highest of 92 % under full-spectrum solar light and nearly 60 % under NIR light. In addition, the catalyst demonstrates good stability during 30 h continuous runs and excellent resistance to SO2 and H2O without noticeable deactivation due to the synergistic effect of P-ATP and CeVO4. This work provides a promising approach for NO removal by taking advantage of the utilization of natural mineral and solar energy of a wider spectrum.
X.L and Z. Wang fabricated the materials and conducted the characterization. H. Shi and D. Dai carried out experimental design. S. Zuo and C.Y performed data analysis.
X.L and C. Ni wrote the paper. In addition, C. Ni polished the language of the paper as native English speaker. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51674043, 51702026) and the Jiangsu International Cooperation Project (BZ2015040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121977.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
